variation.emphasis: Foreground controlling precedent and the standard of review.

advocate_args: no specific contract breach or conditions precedent
rule 9(b) particularity and group pleading defect
decisive_arg: no specific contract breach or conditions precedent

transcript.advocate_opening:
Your Honor, this motion should be granted because the Complaint does not do what the Federal Rules require at the pleading stage: it does not plead facts that make either claim plausible.

Under Rule 12(b)(6), the Court accepts well-pleaded facts as true, but not legal conclusions dressed up as facts. That is the core teaching of *Bell Atlantic v. Twombly* and *Ashcroft v. Iqbal*: a plaintiff must plead enough factual content to move a claim from the merely conceivable to the plausible. And where, as here, fraud is alleged, Rule 9(b) imposes an even stricter standard—particularity as to the circumstances of the alleged fraud.

Starting with the contract claim, the deficiency is fundamental. A breach-of-contract claim requires factual allegations identifying the contract, the specific obligation allegedly breached, the plaintiff’s own performance or satisfaction of any conditions precedent, the breach, and resulting damages. The Complaint does not identify the contractual provision supposedly violated. It does not plead facts showing any condition precedent was satisfied. And it does not allege concrete damages; it asserts harm only in abstract, conclusory terms. That is not a plausibly pleaded contract claim under *Twombly* and *Iqbal*. It is a formulaic recitation of elements without the factual content needed to state a claim.

That omission matters. Without identifying the actual provision breached, neither the Court nor Defendants can test whether the alleged conduct even falls within a contractual duty. Without facts showing satisfaction of conditions precedent, Plaintiff has not plausibly alleged entitlement to performance. And without nonconclusory allegations of damages, Plaintiff has not pleaded a completed claim. At every required step, the Complaint substitutes labels for facts.

The fraud claim is even more defective because Rule 9(b) requires specifics, and the Complaint offers none of them. The rule exists to ensure that a fraud defendant is told the who, what, when, where, and how of the alleged misrepresentation. This Complaint does not specify who made the supposedly false statements, when they were made, where they were made, how they were communicated, or which Defendant said what. Instead, it pleads against all Defendants collectively and asserts, in wholly conclusory fashion, that the conduct was “knowing” and “intentional.” That is precisely the kind of group pleading and rote scienter allegation Rule 9(b) forbids.

And this is not a technical defect that can be overlooked. Particularity is the point of Rule 9(b). If Plaintiff cannot identify the speaker, the statement, the time, the place, or the method of communication, then the Court cannot infer a plausible fraud claim; it can only infer speculation. Likewise, scienter cannot be pleaded by adjective alone. Calling conduct “knowing” or “intentional” does not supply facts giving rise to a strong inference that any Defendant acted with fraudulent intent.

Finally, dismissal with prejudice is warranted at least as to fraud. Plaintiff has already amended once, and the deficiencies described in the motion are not matters of wording or organization; they are substantive omissions. After one amendment, the Complaint still does not identify the basic particulars of the alleged fraud. At some point, the Rules require a plaintiff to plead an actual claim, not a placeholder for one.

So even taking the pleaded facts as true, the Complaint fails under the governing standards: the contract count does not plausibly allege breach, performance, or damages, and the fraud count falls far short of Rule 9(b)’s particularity requirement. The Court should dismiss the Complaint in its entirety, and dismiss the fraud claim with prejudice.

ARGUMENTS: Twombly/Iqbal Plausibility Failure; No Specific Contract Breach or Conditions Precedent; Rule 9(b) Particularity and Group Pleading Defect
transcript.opponent_attack:
Under the governing Rule 12(b)(6) standard, this motion fails at the threshold. The Court must accept the Complaint’s well-pleaded factual allegations as true, draw all reasonable inferences in Plaintiff’s favor, and ask only whether the claims are plausible—not whether Defendants prefer more detail, more proof, or a quotation of contract language they say should have been included. *Twombly* rejected labels and conclusions, but it did not impose fact pleading; *Iqbal* requires plausibility, not evidentiary detail. Defendants’ motion never engages that standard in a complaint-specific way.

On the contract claim, Defendants’ core premise is legally wrong and factually unsupported. Rule 8 does not require a plaintiff to reproduce the breached provision verbatim. A breach-of-contract claim is adequately pleaded where the complaint alleges the existence of the contract, the parties’ obligations, plaintiff’s performance or excuse, defendant’s breach, and resulting damages. That is what the Complaint does. Defendants say Plaintiff “never identifies the specific contractual provision breached,” but they cite no controlling authority holding that failure to quote chapter-and-verse from the agreement defeats plausibility where the contractual duty and the breach are otherwise alleged. That is because no such Rule 8 requirement exists.

Their “conditions precedent” argument is even weaker. Rule 9(c) expressly provides that conditions precedent “may be alleged generally.” So unless the face of the Complaint affirmatively defeats satisfaction or excuse of a condition precedent—and Defendants point to no paragraph that does—this is not a dismissal ground. A Rule 12(b)(6) motion cannot be built on a hypothetical unsatisfied condition precedent untethered to any actual allegation in the pleading. Defendants never identify the supposed condition, never explain why it was unsatisfied, and never show how the Complaint forecloses performance or excuse. That is not a plausibility attack; it is speculation.

The damages argument also misses the standard. At the pleading stage, Plaintiff need not prove damages with mathematical precision. It is enough to allege a non-speculative injury resulting from the breach. The Complaint alleges monetary harm flowing from Defendants’ failure to perform their contractual obligation. Whether the amount is later calculated through discovery is a merits issue, not a Rule 12(b)(6) defect. *Twombly* and *Iqbal* do not require a damages spreadsheet in the complaint.

The fraud argument fares no better under Rule 9(b). Yes, Rule 9(b) requires particularity as to the circumstances of the alleged fraud, but the analysis must still be tethered to the actual allegations. Defendants do not do that work. They do not walk the Complaint paragraph by paragraph and show the absence of the who, what, when, where, and how. Instead, they offer a generic recital that the Complaint does not “specify who made the statements, when or where they were made, or how they were communicated.” That kind of abstract, motion-template attack is not enough—especially where the complaint identifies the substance of the misrepresentation, the participants, the transactional context, and the manner in which Plaintiff was induced. Rule 9(b) demands notice sufficient to permit a response and protect against baseless fraud charges; it does not require maximal detail before discovery, particularly where key facts about internal knowledge and attribution sit with Defendants.

And their scienter argument ignores the text of Rule 9(b): “malice, intent, knowledge, and other conditions of a person’s mind may be alleged generally.” Defendants try to convert a rule that expressly relaxes pleading of scienter into a heightened scienter-proof requirement. That is not the law. If the Complaint alleges facts supporting a plausible inference that the statements were made knowingly or intentionally, Rule 9(b) is satisfied as to mental state.

Most tellingly, Defendants ask for dismissal with prejudice, but that request is indefensible under controlling amendment principles. Even if the Court wanted more granularity on attribution, timing, or wording, those are classic curable pleading matters—not substantive bars. The Supreme Court’s instruction in *Foman v. Davis* is that leave to amend should be freely given absent futility, bad faith, undue delay, or prejudice. Defendants show none of that. “Already amended once” is not a talisman for prejudice where the supposed defects are ones that, by Defendants’ own account, concern pleading detail. At minimum, prejudice is unwarranted.

So the motion should be denied because it does not identify a missing element under Rule 8, does not overcome Rule 9(c) on conditions precedent, does not apply Rule 9(b) in a defendant-specific way, and does not come close to justifying dismissal with prejudice. Defendants are trying to turn federal notice pleading into a demand for evidentiary particularization. That is not *Twombly*, not *Iqbal*, and not Rule 9.

ATTACK: Standards-first complaint-specific failure
transcript.bench_pressure_points: Counsel, your argument is stronger on the fraud claim than on the contract claim, but even there I need more than invocation of *Twombly*, *Iqbal*, and Rule 9(b). On contract, point me to the authority—preferably Second Circuit or New York commercial cases—holding that failure to identify the precise provision, as opposed to the contractual duty allegedly breached, is fatal at Rule 12, and tell me why the allegations of performance or excuse are so deficient that this is not merely a request for greater detail. And if you want dismissal with prejudice of the fraud claim because plaintiff has amended once, I want to know what was previously identified to plaintiff as deficient and why amendment now would be futile rather than simply curative.
transcript.advocate_rebuttal:
On the contract claim, this is not a demand for “chapter-and-verse” pleading; it is the narrower point that a plaintiff must plead facts making the contractual duty plausible, and courts in this Circuit dismiss where the complaint neither identifies the operative provision nor alleges facts from which the duty can be inferred—especially when performance turns on conditions precedent that are not pleaded except as a bare conclusion. See, e.g., *Ixe Banco, S.A. v. MBNA Am. Bank, N.A.*, 2009 WL 3124219, at *4–5 (S.D.N.Y. Sept. 29, 2009)* (dismissing where plaintiff failed to identify contractual language giving rise to the alleged obligation), and *Berman v. Sugo LLC*, 580 F. Supp. 3d 191, 207 (S.D.N.Y. 2022) (breach claim must allege contract, plaintiff’s performance, defendant’s breach, and damages with enough factual content to render the claim plausible). Here, the Complaint does not just omit a quotation; it pleads no nonconclusory facts showing what duty was triggered, that any condition precedent was satisfied or excused, or how damages flowed in anything other than abstract terms.

As to fraud, Rule 9(b) requires the who, what, when, where, and how, and the amended complaint still supplies none of that while relying on group pleading and labels like “knowing” and “intentional”; after one amendment, the persistence of those same core defects supports futility because the problem is not poor drafting but the absence of particularized facts. On this record, dismissal with prejudice is warranted at least as to fraud because amendment would not be curative unless plaintiff can supply facts it has already failed to plead after an earlier opportunity.
transcript.bench_final:
The motion is granted. On this record, the opposition stays at the level of pleading standards in the abstract, while the movant identifies a concrete Rule 8 defect as to the contract claim (no nonconclusory facts showing the contractual duty, satisfaction or excuse of any condition precedent, or nonabstract damages) and an independent Rule 9(b) defect as to fraud (no who, what, when, where, or how, and impermissible group pleading), and those are defects the Court can and should resolve at Rule 12.

DECISIVE: Movant’s claim-specific argument that the complaint does not plead facts making any contractual duty plausible and does not satisfy Rule 9(b)’s particularity requirement, while the opposition never answers those defects with complaint-specific allegations.
